workout.title: Chest Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Chest Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternate Bench Press (high start)
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the lower back; hold a dumbbell in each hand with both arms fully extended above the chest (high start), wrists neutral.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor and a slight natural arch in the lower back; hold a dumbbell in each hand with both arms fully extended above the chest (high start), wrists neutral.
Retract and depress your shoulder blades to create a stable chest platform and keep your core braced to prevent torso rotation.
Slowly lower one dumbbell under control to the mid-chest/nipple line, keeping the elbow at about a 30–45° angle from the torso; the opposite arm remains extended overhead and stable.
Inhale as you lower; pause briefly when the dumbbell reaches chest level, then exhale and press that dumbbell back up to full extension without locking the elbow aggressively.
Once the first arm is fully extended, perform the same controlled descent and press with the opposite arm, alternating reps between arms for the set.
Keep the torso still throughout (do not twist or shift hips), maintain neutral wrist alignment, and choose a weight that allows strict form and smooth, controlled single-arm movement.
exercises.description: A single-arm variation of the flat dumbbell bench press where one dumbbell is lowered and pressed at a time from a high start position to emphasize the pectorals while challenging stability. The non-working arm remains extended overhead while the working arm performs controlled reps.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Fly
exercises.notes: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
instructions: Step 1: Lie supine on a flat bench with feet planted on the floor and a neutral spine; hold a dumbbell in each hand above your chest with palms facing each other and elbows slightly bent.
Step 2: Brace your core and gently retract and depress your shoulder blades to stabilize the shoulder girdle throughout the movement.

Step 3: Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, keeping the same slight bend in your elbows and stopping when your upper arms are about parallel to the floor or you feel a comfortable stretch in the chest (avoid dropping below bench level).

Step 4: Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulders, then exhale and drive the dumbbells back up in the same arc by contracting the pectorals until the weights meet or are close together over your sternum.
Step 5: Maintain controlled tempo (no bouncing), keep elbows fixed at the same angle, and avoid locking the arms at the top; repeat for desired reps.
exercises.description: Lying flat on a bench while holding dumbbells, the dumbbell fly opens the arms in a wide arc and brings them together to isolate and stretch the chest (pectorals). Movement emphasizes a controlled, arcing motion with a slight bend in the elbows to protect the shoulder joint.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Fly
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight and grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip, arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows (no locked joints).
instructions: Sit on the machine with your back firmly against the pad and feet flat on the floor; adjust the seat so the handles are roughly at mid-chest level.
Select an appropriate weight and grasp the handles with a neutral or palms-facing grip, arms out to the sides with a slight bend in the elbows (no locked joints).
Brace your core and keep your chest up and shoulders down and back throughout the movement (seated, back supported).
Exhale and pull the handles together in a smooth arc in front of your chest, leading with the elbows and focusing on squeezing the pectorals at the end range.
Pause briefly at full contraction while maintaining the slight elbow bend and scapula position (do not let shoulders shrug).
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position under control, allowing a stretch in the chest but avoiding excessive shoulder extension.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining consistent tempo and full contact with the back pad.
Common form cues: keep elbows slightly bent, avoid using momentum or leaning forward, and do not let wrists or forearms drive the movement—lead with the chest.
exercises.description: Seated lever fly performed on a chest fly machine to isolate and contract the pectorals by bringing the arms together in front of the torso. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and a controlled arc to emphasize chest contraction.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.title: Back Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Back Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Cable Underhand Pulldown
exercises.notes: Set the seat height and thigh pads so your knees are secured under the pad and select an appropriate weight; attach a straight bar to the high pulley.
instructions: Set the seat height and thigh pads so your knees are secured under the pad and select an appropriate weight; attach a straight bar to the high pulley.
Sit upright on the bench with feet flat, torso tall and slightly braced, then reach up and grasp the bar with an underhand (supinated) grip about shoulder-width apart.
Begin with arms fully extended and scapulae slightly protracted (shoulders relaxed up), inhale, then exhale as you pull the bar down by driving your elbows down and back.
Pull the bar to the upper chest/clavicle area while keeping the torso stable and leaning back only slightly; focus on contracting the lats and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Pause briefly at the bottom, then slowly return the bar under control to the start position with arms nearly straight and a controlled stretch in the lats.
Repeat for the desired reps, avoiding torso momentum or excessive backward lean; keep wrists neutral and core engaged throughout.
exercises.description: Seated cable underhand pulldown (reverse-grip pulldown) is a vertical pulling exercise that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while using a supinated grip to increase biceps involvement. Performed seated at a high pulley, the bar is pulled down to the upper chest with elbows driven down and back.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Row
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a close-grip or double-handle.
instructions: Set the cable pulley to about chest height and attach a close-grip or double-handle.
Stand facing the machine with feet hip-width apart (or a slight staggered stance for balance) and a soft bend in the knees; grasp the handle with a neutral or overhand grip.
Step back until the cable is taut and hinge slightly at the hips so your torso is upright but tilted forward a few degrees; brace your core and keep a neutral spine.
Begin the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction), then drive your elbows straight back toward your sides, keeping them close to the body.
Pull until your hands reach your lower chest/upper abdomen and you feel a full contraction in the lats and upper back; avoid excessive torso lean or momentum.
Pause 0–1 seconds at the top while squeezing the shoulder blades, then slowly extend your arms back to the start under control, maintaining core tension.
Repeat for the desired reps and adjust weight so you can maintain strict form for the full set.
exercises.description: A standing cable rowing movement that targets the lats and mid/upper back by pulling a cable handle toward the torso while maintaining a braced, upright stance. Emphasizes scapular retraction and controlled concentric and eccentric phases.

exercises.name: Cable Low Seated Row with V bar
exercises.notes: Sit tall with a slight forward lean from the hips, chest lifted, core engaged and shoulders relaxed; allow the arms to fully extend so the weight is supported by the arms and scapulae are slightly protracted.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the low cable pulley with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the V‑bar with a neutral (palms facing) grip.

Sit tall with a slight forward lean from the hips, chest lifted, core engaged and shoulders relaxed; allow the arms to fully extend so the weight is supported by the arms and scapulae are slightly protracted.
Initiate the movement by pulling the elbows straight back toward your torso (not flaring wide), driving the elbows past your sides and pulling the V‑bar to your lower chest/upper abdomen.
At the end range, squeeze your shoulder blades together and hold the contraction for 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso stable and upright.
Slowly extend the arms forward in a controlled manner, allowing the scapulae to re‑protract and return to the start position without rounding the lower back.
Breathe out while pulling, inhale while returning; avoid using momentum or leaning excessively backward—keep the motion controlled and driven by the back and arms.
exercises.description: Seated low cable row using a V‑bar is a horizontal pulling exercise that emphasizes the middle/upper back by driving the elbows back and squeezing the shoulder blades, while also engaging the lats and arm muscles. The neutral V‑grip shortens the lever and increases biceps involvement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.title: Shoulder Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Shoulder Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Fly
exercises.notes: Sit near the end of a flat bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart.
instructions: Sit near the end of a flat bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart.

Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is leaning forward (near-parallel to the floor) while keeping a neutral spine and neck; hold a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip and arms hanging down.
Keep a slight bend in the elbows and brace your core to prevent swinging.

Exhale and raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc, leading with your elbows and focusing on contracting the rear delts; lift until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor or in line with your shoulders.
Pause briefly at the top while squeezing the shoulder blades, then slowly lower the weights back to the start under control while inhaling.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a neutral spine and avoiding momentum or torso rotation.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell fly that emphasizes the rear deltoids by horizontally abducting the arms from a forward-leaning seated position. Performed with a slight elbow bend while squeezing the shoulder blades at the top of the movement.

exercises.name: Dumbbel Seated Arnold Press
exercises.notes: Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent and palms facing your body (supinated position).
instructions: Sit on a bench with your feet flat on the floor and your back upright (no excessive arching).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent and palms facing your body (supinated position).
Brace your core and keep your chest up; this is the start position.
Begin the press by rotating your wrists and forearms outward so the palms face forward while driving the dumbbells up in a controlled path.
Press until your arms are nearly locked out overhead, keeping shoulders engaged but not shrugging excessively.
Pause briefly at the top with palms facing forward, then slowly reverse the motion: lower the dumbbells while rotating your palms back toward your body to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, maintaining controlled tempo, full rotation each rep, and steady breathing (exhale up, inhale down).
exercises.description: A seated variation of the Arnold press where dumbbells start by the shoulders with palms facing the body and are rotated outward while pressing overhead to target the delts. The movement emphasizes the front and side heads of the shoulder while requiring triceps and upper-back stability.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Rear Delt Raise
exercises.notes: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
instructions: Stand feet hip-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral (palms facing) grip.
Hinge forward at the hips until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor, keep a neutral spine and knees slightly bent (bent-over position).
Let your arms hang straight down from the shoulders with a slight bend in the elbows; maintain a braced core and avoid rounding the back.
Keeping the elbows slightly bent, raise the dumbbells out to the sides in a wide arc until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the ground (level with the shoulders).
Squeeze the rear delts and shoulder blades together at the top for a brief pause, avoid shrugging the shoulders up toward the ears.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start under control, resisting any momentum; repeat for desired reps.
Keep the movement strict: use lighter weight if you must swing the torso or bend the elbows excessively, and breathe out on the lift and in on the lower.
exercises.description: A bent-over dumbbell raise that isolates the rear (posterior) deltoids by lifting the arms out to the sides while the torso is hinged forward. It emphasizes shoulder horizontal abduction and scapular retraction with a controlled tempo.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.title: Arm Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Arm Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Cable Alternate Triceps Extension
exercises.notes: Set a single handle on a high or mid cable pulley and select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Set a single handle on a high or mid cable pulley and select an appropriate weight.
Stand upright facing the machine with feet about hip-width apart and grasp the handle with one hand (palm facing down or neutral).
Raise your elbow so the upper arm is vertical or slightly forward and tucked close to your head; your forearm should start bent with the hand near ear/temple (standing position).
Keep your upper arm and elbow fixed in place (avoid letting the elbow drift); brace your core and maintain a neutral spine.
Extend the forearm by straightening the elbow until the arm is fully extended, squeezing the triceps at the end of the movement.
Slowly return to the start position with control, resisting the weight on the way back.
Complete the desired reps, then switch sides and repeat, maintaining the same posture and controlled tempo for each rep.
exercises.description: Standing single-arm cable triceps extension that isolates the triceps by extending the forearm against a cable; performed alternating arms for unilateral focus and balance. Use a high or mid pulley and keep the upper arm stationary to maximize triceps tension.

exercises.name: EZ Barbell Lying Triceps Extension
exercises.notes: Setup: Load the EZ bar with an appropriate weight and place it on the rack.
instructions: Setup: Load the EZ bar with an appropriate weight and place it on the rack. Lie supine (flat on your back) on a flat bench with feet flat on the floor for stability.
Grip the bar with a shoulder-width or slightly narrower angled grip on the EZ bar and lift it off the rack so your arms are fully extended above your chest (bar over your forehead/chest line).

Positioning: Keep your upper arms perpendicular to the floor (elbows pointing toward the ceiling) and wrists neutral; avoid flaring elbows outward—upper arms should remain stationary throughout the movement.

Lowering phase: Inhale and slowly bend only at the elbows, lowering the bar in a controlled arc toward your forehead or just behind your head until your forearms are near parallel to the floor or you feel a full triceps stretch.
Pressing phase: Exhale and extend the elbows to press the bar back up to the starting position with the arms nearly straight but avoiding full joint lockout if preferred for tension.
Repetitions: Repeat for the desired number of reps with a controlled tempo; keep the core engaged and avoid arching the lower back.
Safety notes: Use a spotter for heavy sets, control the descent to avoid striking the face, and reduce weight if elbows drift or upper arms move excessively.
exercises.description: A supine arm exercise performed with an EZ curl bar that isolates the triceps by extending the elbows from a bent position; commonly called skull crushers or lying triceps extensions. It emphasizes the long and lateral heads of the triceps while the pectorals and forearms assist with stabilization and grip.

exercises.name: EZ Barbell Close Grip Preacher Curl
exercises.notes: Adjust the preacher bench seat so your chest/upper arms rest comfortably against the pad and your armpits sit at the top edge of the pad; sit with feet flat on the floor.
instructions: Adjust the preacher bench seat so your chest/upper arms rest comfortably against the pad and your armpits sit at the top edge of the pad; sit with feet flat on the floor.
Load an appropriate weight on an EZ bar and grasp the inner (close) section of the bar with a supinated/semi-supinated grip (palms facing up), hands closer than shoulder width.
Start with arms extended down and elbows resting on the pad; maintain a slight, natural bend in the elbow (do not lock joint) and keep the torso still.
Exhale and curl the bar upward by flexing the elbows, driving only from the forearm—keep the upper arms pressed to the pad and avoid shoulder or torso movement.
Pause and squeeze the biceps briefly at the top of the movement with the bar near shoulder level.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar back to the start position under control until the forearms are nearly extended while maintaining tension in the biceps.
Repeat for the desired reps; avoid swinging the body, using momentum, or lifting the elbows off the pad to preserve isolation and protect the joints.
exercises.description: Seated preacher-style curl using a close grip on an EZ bar that isolates the biceps by bracing the upper arms on a sloped pad and removing torso momentum. Performed with a controlled concentric curl and slow eccentric lower to maximize biceps tension.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

workout.title: Leg Day - BODYBUILD
workout.description: Leg Day workout focusing on bodybuild training.

exercises.name: Sled 45 degrees Leg Press
exercises.notes: Set up: Sit and then lie supine on the sled bench which is angled at 45 degrees; place your feet shoulder-width on the middle of the footplate with toes pointing slightly outward.
instructions: Set up: Sit and then lie supine on the sled bench which is angled at 45 degrees; place your feet shoulder-width on the middle of the footplate with toes pointing slightly outward.
Grip the side handles and ensure your lower back and hips are pressed firmly against the backpad; unlock the sled safety/locks if present.
Start position: Bend your knees to bring your thighs toward your chest until knees are about 90 degrees, keeping feet flat on the plate and knees tracking over toes.
Press: Exhale and drive through your heels and mid-foot to extend your knees and hips, pushing the footplate away until legs are nearly straight but do not lock the knees at full extension.
Return: Inhale and slowly control the sled back down by bending the knees, lowering the weight under tension until your knees reach about 90 degrees again.
Repetitions: Repeat for the desired number of reps with controlled tempo; avoid bouncing at the bottom or using momentum to lock the knees.
Safety cues: Keep your lower back pressed into the pad throughout, avoid lifting hips off the pad, and keep knees aligned with feet. Rerack/lock the sled before exiting.
exercises.description: A 45-degree sled leg press performed supine on an inclined sled platform where you push the footplate away using the legs. The movement primarily extends the knees to target the quadriceps while the glutes, hamstrings, and calves assist and stabilize.

exercises.name: Smith Deadlift
exercises.notes: Set the Smith machine bar to just below mid‑shin height and load an appropriate weight; lock safety stops if available.
instructions: Set the Smith machine bar to just below mid‑shin height and load an appropriate weight; lock safety stops if available.
Stand facing the bar with feet about hip‑width apart and the bar positioned over the middle of your feet; adopt a standing position (not kneeling or prone).
Bend at the hips and knees to reach the bar, keeping a neutral spine and chest lifted; grip the bar with an overhand or mixed grip just outside your knees.
Initiate the lift by driving through your heels and extending your hips, keeping the bar close to your legs and maintaining a straight, braced torso.
Fully extend the hips at the top and squeeze the glutes briefly without overarching the lower back; exhale as you complete the upward phase.
Lower the bar under control by hinging at the hips and allowing the knees to bend slightly until the bar returns to the starting position near mid‑shin.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining a neutral spine and steady tempo; rack the bar safely when finished.
exercises.description: Smith machine deadlift is a hip‑hinge pulling exercise performed on a guided bar that primarily develops the glutes and posterior chain while training hip extension and back stability.

exercises.name: Barbell Floor Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Load a barbell and position it across the upper back/trapezius, not the neck; grip the bar just outside shoulder width with a firm hold.
instructions: Load a barbell and position it across the upper back/trapezius, not the neck; grip the bar just outside shoulder width with a firm hold.
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and weight evenly distributed on the mid-foot; keep a slight bend in the knees (not locked).
Brace your core and maintain an upright torso; focus on keeping shoulders down and the bar stable on your traps/delts.
Slowly raise both heels by pushing through the balls of your feet until you reach full plantarflexion (stand on your toes); contract the calves at the top and hold for 1–2 seconds.
Lower your heels back to the floor in a controlled manner until the soles are flat, feeling a stretch in the calves.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the ascent and inhaling on the descent.
If balance is an issue, perform the movement near a wall or use a spotter; avoid bouncing or using excessive forward trunk lean to lift the weight.
exercises.description: Standing barbell calf raise performed with a barbell resting on the upper back to load the lower legs; raise the heels by plantarflexing the ankles, pause at the top, then lower with control. This exercise primarily isolates the gastrocnemius/soleus (calves) while the upper back and shoulders stabilize the load.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Step-up
exercises.notes: Stand facing a sturdy bench or box (~knee height) with a dumbbell in each hand held at your sides, feet hip-width apart and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand facing a sturdy bench or box (~knee height) with a dumbbell in each hand held at your sides, feet hip-width apart and core braced (standing position).
Place one whole foot firmly on the bench so the midfoot and heel are secure and the knee tracks over the toes (all fours not used).

Drive through the heel of the foot on the bench, extend the knee and hip of the stepping leg to lift your body up until that leg is straight and you are upright on the bench (standing on the bench); keep torso upright and avoid leaning forward.
Optionally bring the trailing foot up to the bench briefly or keep it just off the bench for greater single-leg demand; keep dumbbells close to your sides and shoulders relaxed (upright standing).
Lower yourself slowly and under control by flexing the hip and knee of the same leg until the trailing foot returns to the floor (return to starting standing position).
Repeat for the desired reps on one leg, then switch legs. Maintain a neutral spine, braced abs, and avoid pushing off strongly with the trailing leg—focus on the working leg doing the work.
Breathe out as you press up and inhale as you step down; choose bench height and dumbbell load so you can control each repetition without rocking or using momentum.
exercises.description: A single-leg clinic-style step onto a raised bench while holding dumbbells to load the working leg; primarily develops knee-extension strength and builds thigh muscle. Performed standing, it also recruits the glutes and hamstrings for hip extension and core muscles for stability.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Rack Lunge
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and the barbell racked across the front of your shoulders (front-rack) with elbows high and upper arms roughly parallel to the floor; grip the bar with either a clean grip or cross-arm position.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart and the barbell racked across the front of your shoulders (front-rack) with elbows high and upper arms roughly parallel to the floor; grip the bar with either a clean grip or cross-arm position.
Brace your core, keep your chest tall and shoulders back to maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Take a controlled long step forward with one foot (standing position to forward lunge), landing on the heel of the front foot.

Lower your body by bending both knees until the front thigh is approximately parallel to the floor and the back knee is close to the ground (about 90° at the front knee); keep the front knee tracking over the toes and avoid letting it collapse inward.
Pause briefly at the bottom while maintaining an upright torso and tight core; keep the bar stable on the front shoulders by keeping elbows up.
Drive through the front heel to push yourself back to the starting standing position, fully extending the front hip and knee.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, then switch legs and perform the same number of repetitions on the opposite side.
Safety notes: choose a weight that allows you to keep an upright torso and high elbows; if balance is difficult, reduce the load or perform stationary split squats until comfortable.
exercises.description: A loaded lunge performed from a front-rack barbell position that primarily targets the quadriceps while also engaging the glutes, hamstrings and core for stability. The bar is held on the front shoulders with elbows high while stepping forward and lowering into a controlled lunge.

exercises.name: Barbell Floor Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Load a barbell and position it across the upper back/trapezius, not the neck; grip the bar just outside shoulder width with a firm hold.
instructions: Load a barbell and position it across the upper back/trapezius, not the neck; grip the bar just outside shoulder width with a firm hold.
Stand upright with feet about hip-width apart and weight evenly distributed on the mid-foot; keep a slight bend in the knees (not locked).

Slowly raise both heels by pushing through the balls of your feet until you reach full plantarflexion (stand on your toes); contract the calves at the top and hold for 1–2 seconds.
Lower your heels back to the floor in a controlled manner until the soles are flat, feeling a stretch in the calves.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, breathing out on the ascent and inhaling on the descent.
If balance is an issue, perform the movement near a wall or use a spotter; avoid bouncing or using excessive forward trunk lean to lift the weight.

This exercise primarily isolates the gastrocnemius/soleus (calves) while the upper back and shoulders stabilize the load.

workout.description: Upper Body workout focusing on strength training.

exercises.name: Cable Lateral Pulldown with Mag Grip
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated (kneeling not required); select an appropriate weight on the stack.
instructions: Adjust the seat height and knee pads so your thighs are secured under the pads when seated (kneeling not required); select an appropriate weight on the stack.
Attach the mag grip (neutral/V handle) to the high pulley and sit facing the machine with feet flat, knees bent at ~90°, and torso upright.
Reach up and grasp the mag grip with a neutral grip (palms facing each other) and arms fully extended overhead; brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly.
Initiate the movement by pulling your elbows down and back, bringing the handle toward the upper chest/upper sternum while keeping your torso mostly upright (avoid excessive leaning back).
Pause briefly at the bottom with a full lat contraction and elbows tucked, then slowly control the cable as you extend your arms back to the start position until the lats are stretched.
Keep neck neutral, avoid shrugging the shoulders during the pull, and breathe out as you pull down and inhale as you return.
Perform the desired number of repetitions with a smooth, controlled tempo; reduce weight if you must use momentum or excessive torso lean.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a mag (V/neutral) grip that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while pulling the handle down in a controlled, slightly lateral path. Maintain an upright seated position and drive the elbows down and back to maximize back contraction.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Palms In Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip-width apart, knees slightly bent and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing each other and elbows tucked slightly forward.
Keep your chest up and spine neutral; avoid overarching the lower back by engaging the abs throughout the movement.
Exhale and press both dumbbells straight up until the arms are nearly extended overhead (do not fully lock the elbows).
Keep the palms facing each other for the entire press and maintain a steady, controlled path overhead.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back down to shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining posture and avoiding forward/backward rocking of the torso.
exercises.description: A standing neutral-grip (palms-facing) dumbbell press that drives the weights overhead from shoulder height to target the shoulder muscles. The palms-in grip reduces shoulder rotation and emphasizes the delts while engaging the triceps and upper chest for assistance.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Barbell Full Squat
exercises.notes: Stand upright (standing) under a loaded barbell set at about mid-chest height in a squat rack; step under the bar so it rests across your upper traps/shoulders (not on the neck).
instructions: Stand upright (standing) under a loaded barbell set at about mid-chest height in a squat rack; step under the bar so it rests across your upper traps/shoulders (not on the neck).
Grip the bar just outside shoulder width, lift the chest, pull the shoulder blades together, and unrack the bar by straightening the legs and stepping back to clear the rack.
Position feet shoulder-width to slightly wider with toes pointed slightly outward; establish a stable stance with weight distributed through the midfoot and heel.
Brace your core (abs) and keep a neutral spine; inhale before you descend to maintain intra-abdominal pressure.
Begin the descent by pushing the hips back and bending the knees simultaneously, keeping the chest up and spine neutral — descend until your hips drop below parallel (full squat depth).
Ensure knees track over the toes and do not collapse inward; keep the weight on the heels/midfoot and maintain even foot contact with the floor.
Pause briefly at the bottom if desired, then drive forcefully through the heels to extend the hips and knees and return to a fully upright position, exhaling as you ascend.
Control the movement on the way up and down; use safety pins or a spotter when training heavy and avoid rounding the lower back at any point.
exercises.description: A barbell back full squat performed to below-parallel depth; primarily loads the quadriceps while also recruiting the glutes, hamstrings and core for stabilization. The lifter descends with the bar on the upper back and then drives back up to standing.

exercises.name: Lever Glute Press
exercises.notes: Set the lever machine to the desired weight and adjust the chest pad height so your torso is upright and supported when gripping the handles.
instructions: Set the lever machine to the desired weight and adjust the chest pad height so your torso is upright and supported when gripping the handles.
Stand facing the machine with your chest pressed into the pad, both hands holding the handles for support, and feet on the platform or footpads as required by the machine.
Position the working leg so the foot is centered on the footplate with the knee slightly bent (single‑leg variation) or place both feet if using both legs; keep the non‑working leg clear of the path.
Brace your core (abs) and maintain a neutral spine and fixed chest on the pad; avoid leaning forward or arching the lower back.
Drive the working foot backward and slightly upward by extending the hip, contracting the glute until the leg is nearly straight or you reach a controlled end range of hip extension.
Pause briefly at full contraction, squeezing the glute, then slowly return the lever to the start position with control, resisting the weight rather than letting it snap back.
Complete the prescribed reps, then switch legs and repeat. Keep movement smooth and controlled throughout; reduce range or weight if you feel lumbar strain.
exercises.description: A chest‑supported, single‑leg hip extension performed on a lever machine that drives the footplate backward to extend the hip and contract the glute. It develops unilateral hip power and isolates the glute while the core stabilizes the torso.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bench Press
exercises.notes: Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
instructions: Sit on the edge of a flat bench with a dumbbell resting on each thigh.
Lie back so you are supine on the bench, feet flat on the floor, knees bent, and maintain a neutral spine with a slight natural arch; retract and squeeze your shoulder blades together.
Use your legs and core to help bring the dumbbells up to chest height so your upper arms are about 45 degrees from your torso and palms are facing forward (or slightly inward if preferred).

Inhale, then slowly lower the dumbbells in a controlled manner until your elbows are roughly level with the bench or just below chest level, keeping wrists neutral and elbows tracking slightly tucked.

Pause briefly at the bottom without relaxing the shoulder blade position, then exhale and press the dumbbells upward and slightly together until your arms are almost fully extended — avoid locking the elbows.
Control the descent back to the starting position and repeat for the desired number of repetitions.
To finish the set, bring the dumbbells safely to your knees, sit up, and set them down or have a spotter assist if using heavy weight.
exercises.description: The dumbbell bench press is a supine pressing movement performed on a flat bench that primarily develops the pectorals while also recruiting the triceps and delts for elbow and shoulder extension. Dumbbells allow a greater range of motion and require more stabilization from the forearms and serratus anterior.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Press Squat
exercises.notes: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
instructions: Start standing with feet shoulder-width apart, toes pointing slightly outward and a dumbbell held vertically at chest in a goblet grip (hands around the head/handle), elbows close to your body.
Brace your core, pull your shoulders down and back, keep chest up and eyes forward throughout the movement.
Inhale and hinge at the hips while bending the knees to lower into a squat — push hips back and let knees track over toes until thighs are at least parallel to the floor.
Drive through your heels and midfoot to stand back up, exhaling as you rise and maintaining a strong upright torso.
Once standing, press the dumbbell overhead (or slightly forward depending on comfort), extending the elbows and engaging delts/pectorals; exhale during the press.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the goblet position at your chest with control, resetting your brace before the next descent.
Repeat for the desired reps, keeping movements steady, knees tracking toes and avoiding rounded lower back or knees collapsing inward.
exercises.description: A standing compound movement that combines a goblet-style squat with a dumbbell press; it primarily works the quads while also engaging the chest, shoulders and arms. Perform by squatting from a goblet hold and then pressing the weight as you stand.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.
Take full rest between sets for neural recovery.
Prioritize form over weight - quality reps build strength.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Alternate Side Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, core braced and a neutral spine. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, core braced and a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand in the racked position at shoulder height (elbows bent, dumbbells near the sides of the head), palms facing each other or slightly forward.
Tighten your abs and press one dumbbell overhead and slightly out to the side until the arm is fully extended but not locked out; exhale during the press.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulder engaged and the torso upright—avoid leaning or arching the lower back.
Slowly lower the dumbbell back to the racked shoulder position under control while inhaling.
Repeat the press with the opposite arm, alternating sides for the desired number of repetitions while maintaining a braced core and steady tempo.
Keep wrists neutral, shoulders down (not shrugged), and avoid rotating the torso—use the working shoulder and triceps to move the weight.
exercises.description: Standing single-arm (alternating) dumbbell press where each dumbbell is pressed from a racked shoulder position up and slightly out to the side; primarily targets the shoulder (delts) while the triceps and chest assist and the core stabilizes.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Single Arm Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat (and chest pad if available) so the handle is reachable with your arm fully extended; select an appropriate weight and place feet flat on the foot platform. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat (and chest pad if available) so the handle is reachable with your arm fully extended; select an appropriate weight and place feet flat on the foot platform.
Sit upright with a neutral spine, chest lightly against the pad or upright on the seat, feet planted, and grasp the single handle with a neutral or overhand grip.
Begin with the working arm fully extended and the shoulder relaxed; brace your core and prevent torso rotation.
Exhale and pull the handle by driving the elbow straight back toward your hip, leading with the elbow and actively retracting the shoulder blade.
Pause 1–2 seconds at the top and squeeze the lats and upper back while keeping the torso still.
Inhale and slowly control the handle back to the start, fully extending the arm without collapsing the shoulder.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch sides; maintain a controlled tempo and avoid using momentum or twisting the torso.
exercises.description: A unilateral seated row performed on a lever machine that emphasizes the lats by pulling a single handle back while keeping the torso stable. Each rep focuses on driving the elbow toward the hip and retracting the shoulder blade for a strong concentric contraction.

exercises.name: Lever Pec Deck Fly
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat height so the handles/pads are level with the mid-chest; sit upright with your back flat against the pad and feet flat on the floor.
Grasp the handles or place your forearms against the pads with a slight bend in the elbows; start with arms out to the sides and shoulder blades pulled back and down.
Exhale and drive the handles forward and together in a smooth arc by contracting the pectorals, keeping the elbows slightly bent and wrists neutral.
Pause briefly when the hands/handles meet or are directly in front of your chest, feeling the squeeze in the pectorals.
Inhale and slowly allow the levers to return to the start position under control, maintaining shoulder blade stability and avoiding rapid letting go or trunk leaning.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions; use a weight that allows full control of both concentric and eccentric phases without excessive shoulder or back movement.
exercises.description: Seated machine chest fly performed on a lever/pec-deck: you bring the arms together in front of the chest against resistance to isolate the pectoral muscles. The movement uses a slight elbow bend and controlled arc to emphasize the inner chest.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Lever Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the machine with your back fully against the pad and feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90 degrees. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Sit upright on the machine with your back fully against the pad and feet flat on the floor, knees bent at ~90 degrees.
Adjust the seat height so the handles are at or just below shoulder level when your elbows are bent.
Grip the handles with a neutral or overhand grip, wrists in line with forearms, and elbows pointed slightly forward (not flared straight out).
Brace your core and keep your head and upper back pressed against the pad throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the handles upward in a controlled arc until your arms are nearly extended; avoid locking the elbows at the top.
Inhale and lower the handles slowly back to the start position with control until your elbows are at approximately shoulder level.
Use a weight that allows smooth, controlled reps without excessive back arching; stop the set if you cannot maintain form.
exercises.description: A seated machine (lever) shoulder press where you push handles upward from shoulder level to work the delts. The machine stabilizes the path while triceps and upper back/traps assist and stabilize the movement.

exercises.name: Lever Front Pulldown
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat and thigh pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the pads when seated upright. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat and thigh pads so your feet are flat on the floor and your thighs are secured under the pads when seated upright.
Sit upright (not reclined) with a neutral spine, grasp the overhead handles with a pronated (palms facing away) or neutral grip depending on the machine, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Begin with your arms extended overhead and your shoulder blades elevated; brace your core and keep chest up.
Exhale and pull the handles down in a smooth arc toward the upper chest by driving your elbows down and back, focusing on squeezing the lats and pinching the shoulder blades together.
Avoid excessive torso lean or using momentum; keep the movement controlled and lead with the elbows rather than pulling with the hands.
Pause briefly at the bottom with the handles near your chest while maintaining scapular retraction and lat tension.
Inhale and slowly return the handles to the start position with a controlled motion until your arms are extended overhead and your shoulder blades are slightly apart.
Repeat for the desired reps. Use a weight that allows full, controlled range of motion and maintain steady breathing throughout (exhale pulling down, inhale returning up).
exercises.description: Seated lever front pulldown performed on a machine where you pull handles from overhead down in front of you; it primarily targets the lats while engaging the upper back and arm muscles. Maintain a tall torso and lead the movement with the elbows to maximize back activation.

exercises.name: Lever Single Arm Shoulder Press (plate loaded)
exercises.notes: Adjust the seat so the handle lines up with your shoulder height when seated; load the desired plates on the lever. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Adjust the seat so the handle lines up with your shoulder height when seated; load the desired plates on the lever.
Sit upright with your back firmly against the pad, feet flat on the floor, and grasp the single-arm lever handle with an overhand (or neutral) grip.
Start with the working elbow bent about 80–100 degrees and the upper arm close to your ear/shoulder; keep your wrist neutral.
Brace your core and keep a neutral spine; do not arch your lower back or lean away from the machine.
Exhale and press the lever upward along the machine's path until your arm is nearly straight overhead; avoid locking the elbow and shrugging the shoulder.
Pause briefly at the top under control, then inhale and slowly lower the lever back to the starting position following the same path.
Perform the desired reps, then switch sides; throughout the set maintain steady tempo, neutral head position, and avoid twisting the torso.
exercises.description: A single-arm, plate-loaded lever variation of the seated shoulder press that primarily works the delts while the lever arm guides the pressing path. Performed seated with a single hand pressing a plate-loaded lever overhead for unilateral shoulder strength and stability.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Barbell Front Squat
exercises.notes: Set the barbell in a rack at about shoulder height. Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set the barbell in a rack at about shoulder height. Step under the bar and position it across the front of your shoulders (front of delts/upper chest) using either a clean (front-rack) grip or a cross-arm hold.
Stand up to unrack the bar, take one or two small steps back, and place your feet about shoulder-width apart with toes pointed slightly out.
Raise your elbows so your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor and brace your abs to keep the torso upright throughout the movement.
Inhale, push your hips back slightly and bend at the knees to descend into a squat; keep your chest up, elbows high, and knees tracking over your toes.
Continue lowering until your thighs reach at least parallel to the floor (or slightly below if mobility allows) while maintaining a neutral spine.
Exhale and drive through your heels and midfoot to extend your knees and hips, returning to the standing start position with control.
Maintain tight abs and high elbows on every rep; avoid letting the knees cave inward or the torso fold forward. Rack the bar safely when finished.
exercises.description: A front-loaded barbell squat where the barbell is held across the front of the shoulders, emphasizing upright torso and knee extension to target the quadriceps. Performed by descending into a squat and driving back up while keeping elbows high and core braced.

exercises.name: Barbell Forward Backward Lunge
exercises.notes: Setup: Rack the barbell at about shoulder height and position it across your upper traps (high-bar). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Setup: Rack the barbell at about shoulder height and position it across your upper traps (high-bar). Grasp the bar slightly wider than shoulder-width, step back from the rack and stand tall with feet hip-width apart (standing).
Brace your core and keep chest upright, eyes forward and a slight bend in the knees to maintain tension.

Forward lunge: Step one foot forward about 2–3 feet (depending on leg length) and lower into a lunge until the front thigh is roughly parallel to the floor and the back knee is close to the ground (both knees about 90°). Keep the front knee tracking over the toes and weight distributed through the front heel.
Drive through the front heel to return to the start position, fully extending the front hip and knee while maintaining an upright torso.

Without pausing (or after a brief reset), perform a backward/reverse lunge with the same leg: step that same foot straight back and lower until both knees are about 90°, keeping the front knee above the ankle and torso upright.
Push through the front heel to return to standing. That completes one forward+backward rep for that leg.
Repeat for the desired reps, then switch legs. Maintain even, controlled tempo; avoid letting the front knee collapse inward or the torso lean forward excessively.
Safety notes: Use a spotter or safety pins when racking heavy loads, keep the bar on the traps (not the neck), and choose a step distance that allows comfortable 90° knee angles.
exercises.description: A loaded lunge variation where a barbell is carried on the upper back while performing a forward step lunge followed by a backward/reverse lunge, targeting the quadriceps with added work for the glutes and hamstrings. This drill also challenges balance and core stability.

exercises.name: Barbell Standing Leg Calf Raise
exercises.notes: Set up a squat rack at an appropriate height and load the barbell; unrack the bar and place it securely across your upper back/traps (not on your neck). Train to muscular failure.
instructions: Set up a squat rack at an appropriate height and load the barbell; unrack the bar and place it securely across your upper back/traps (not on your neck).

Step onto a raised, stable platform or aerobic step so the balls of your feet are on the edge and your heels can drop freely; stand upright with feet about hip-to-shoulder width and toes pointing forward.
Grip the bar with a comfortable overhand grip to maintain control; engage your abs and keep your chest up and spine neutral throughout (standing position).
Keep knees straight but not locked (a slight soft bend) and maintain weight balanced over the balls of your feet.
Exhale and raise your heels by plantarflexing at the ankle, lifting as high as possible onto your toes and pausing 1–2 seconds at the top to maximize contraction.
Inhale and slowly lower your heels down below the platform level until you feel a stretch in the calves; control the descent — do not drop quickly or bounce.
Repeat for the desired repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and full range of motion. Use a spotter or safety pins in the rack if using heavy weight.
When finished, carefully step down from the platform and re-rack the bar in the squat rack.
exercises.description: Standing barbell calf raise performed from a raised platform with a barbell across the upper back; rise onto the toes to concentrically contract the calves, then lower the heels below the platform for a full stretch. This is a standing, weight-bearing isolation exercise for the calves with upper-back and core stabilization.

tips: Every working set should reach true muscular failure.
Use slow, controlled tempo throughout the movement.
Allow full recovery between exercises - this is not cardio.

exercises.name: Close Grip Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and grasp it with a close, underhand (palms facing you / supinated) grip roughly shoulder-width or slightly narrower; thumbs can wrap around the bar.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and grasp it with a close, underhand (palms facing you / supinated) grip roughly shoulder-width or slightly narrower; thumbs can wrap around the bar.
Step or jump up so you are hanging with arms fully extended, legs either bent at the knees or crossed behind you to avoid touching the floor; maintain a slight hollow-body position with core engaged.
Begin the rep by depressing and slightly retracting the shoulder blades (set the scapula) to engage the lats before pulling with the arms.
Pull your body upward by driving the elbows down and back, leading with the chest and squeezing the lats and upper back until your chin clears the bar.
Pause briefly at the top while keeping the torso stable and the biceps engaged.
Lower yourself slowly and under control back to a full dead hang with arms extended, maintaining tension in the lats and core throughout the descent.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, avoiding kipping or excessive swinging; breathe out while pulling up and inhale while lowering.
exercises.description: A close-grip chin-up is an underhand (supinated) pull-up variation performed with a narrow grip that emphasizes the lats while placing significant demand on the biceps and forearms. The movement involves pulling the body up until the chin clears the bar, then lowering under control to a full hang.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Lying Hammer Press
exercises.notes: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and knees bent; keep your back neutral and shoulder blades gently retracted.
instructions: Lie supine on a flat bench with your feet flat on the floor and knees bent; keep your back neutral and shoulder blades gently retracted.
Pick up a pair of dumbbells and hold them at chest level with a neutral (palms facing each other / hammer) grip and elbows bent about 45 degrees from your torso.
Brace your core, keep your wrists neutral, and press the dumbbells upward in a controlled path until your arms are nearly fully extended but not locked out.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining the neutral grip and stable shoulder position.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells under control back to the starting position at chest level, keeping elbows from flaring excessively.
Repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on steady, controlled motion and exhaling during the press and inhaling during the lowering phase.
Maintain scapular stability throughout; avoid overarching the lower back or letting the shoulders roll forward.
exercises.description: Supine dumbbell press using a neutral (hammer) grip to press weights from chest to full arm extension; emphasizes the chest while reducing shoulder rotation. Performed on a flat bench with feet planted and core braced.

exercises.name: Shoulder Grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grasp it with an overhand (palms facing away) grip approximately shoulder-width apart.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grasp it with an overhand (palms facing away) grip approximately shoulder-width apart.
Step or jump up so your feet are off the floor and your body is hanging straight down (dead hang) with arms fully extended; you may cross your ankles or bend the knees for comfort.
Engage your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to remove slack before initiating the pull (scapular pull).
Exhale and pull your elbows down and back, driving your chest toward the bar while keeping the torso as vertical as possible; lead with the elbows rather than the hands.
Continue pulling until your chin or upper chest reaches or clears the bar, squeezing the lats and upper back at the top for a brief pause.
Inhale and lower yourself slowly and under control back to a full dead hang with arms fully extended, maintaining tension in the lats and core.
Avoid kipping, excessive swinging, or shrugging the shoulders; keep movements controlled and use full range of motion for each rep.
If you cannot complete strict reps, use a band for assistance or perform negatives (slow controlled descent) to build strength.
exercises.description: A shoulder-width overhand pull-up that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi with upper-back and arm assistance. Begin from a dead hang and pull the torso upward until the chest approaches the bar, then lower under control.

exercises.name: Hammer Grip Pull-up on Dip Cage
exercises.notes: Stand beneath the dip cage and grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), hands about shoulder-width apart; step or jump up until arms are fully extended and you are in a full hang.
instructions: Stand beneath the dip cage and grasp the parallel/neutral handles with a hammer grip (palms facing each other), hands about shoulder-width apart; step or jump up until arms are fully extended and you are in a full hang. Cross or bend your legs behind you if needed to avoid touching the floor.
Brace your core and begin by depressing and retracting the shoulder blades (scapular pull) to engage the lats before bending the elbows.

Pull your body upward by driving the elbows down and back toward your ribs, leading with the chest until your chin clears the bar or your chest reaches the handles; keep the torso relatively upright and avoid excessive swinging or kipping.
Hold the top position briefly with shoulders packed (down and back), then slowly lower yourself under control back to a full dead hang with arms fully extended.
Breathe out as you pull up and inhale as you lower; maintain a steady tempo and repeat for the desired number of reps, focusing on lat contraction rather than momentum.
exercises.description: A neutral (hammer) grip pull-up performed on a dip/pull-up station that emphasizes the lats while reducing strain on the shoulders by using palms-facing-each-other handles. Performed from a dead hang and pulling the chest toward the bar with controlled descent.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Bent Arm Lateral raise
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides.
Bend your elbows to about 90 degrees so the dumbbells are in front of your thighs and palms face down or slightly toward each other.
Keeping the elbows bent and the torso still, raise your forearms and elbows out to the sides until your upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor (elbows stay higher than the hands).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining shoulder blade stability; avoid excessive shrugging or leaning the torso.
Slowly lower the weights back to the start under control, keeping tension on the delts and avoiding swinging.
Repeat for the desired reps, maintaining neutral spine and steady breathing throughout.
exercises.description: A standing lateral raise variation performed with the elbows bent to shorten the lever arm and focus tension on the lateral delts. Lift the dumbbells out to the sides until the upper arms are roughly parallel to the floor, then lower with control.

tips: Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.
Focus on breathing patterns - exhale on exertion.
Keep transitions between exercises quick.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Overhead Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
instructions: Stand tall with feet hip-width apart, knees slightly bent, and core braced (standing position).
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with palms facing forward or neutral and elbows slightly in front of the body.
Keep the spine neutral and ribcage down to avoid hyperextending the lower back; squeeze the abs throughout.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled path until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead without locking the elbows.
Pause briefly at the top with shoulders stacked over hips and the head neutral (you may slightly move your head back to allow the dumbbells to pass), keeping shoulder blades stable.
Inhale and lower the dumbbells slowly back to the starting shoulder-height position under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, avoiding excessive torso lean or lower-back arching; if needed, use a lighter weight to maintain good form.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell overhead press is a compound shoulder exercise where you press dumbbells from shoulder height to full arm extension overhead, primarily working the delts while engaging the triceps and upper back for support and stabilization.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Shoulder Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at shoulder height with elbows bent ~90°, palms facing forward or slightly inward and wrists neutral.
Inhale, keep the spine neutral and shoulders down away from the ears.
Exhale and press both dumbbells upward in a controlled motion until the arms are nearly fully extended overhead (avoid locking the elbows).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and keeping the torso stable.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to shoulder height with control to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining a steady tempo and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Incline Alternative Fly
exercises.notes: Set an adjustable bench to a 30–45° incline and sit with a dumbbell in each hand.

Hold the dumbbells with a neutral or slightly pronated grip and press them together over your upper chest with a small bend in both elbows; this is the start position.
Keep your shoulder blades lightly retracted and braced throughout the set to protect the shoulder joint.

Lower one dumbbell in a slow, controlled arc out to the side (maintain the same elbow bend) until you feel a stretch in the upper chest; keep the opposite arm stationary and slightly elevated above the chest.
Pause briefly at the bottom for tension on the pectorals, then drive that dumbbell back up in the same arc to meet the elevated dumbbell above the chest, squeezing the pectorals at the top.
Alternate sides each repetition, maintaining control, a constant elbow angle, and avoiding bouncing or letting the lowered elbow drop behind the plane of your torso.
Breathe in as you lower the weight and exhale as you bring it back up. Use a weight that allows strict form and full control for the full range of motion.

exercises.name: Cable Straight Back Seated Row
exercises.notes: Select an appropriate weight, reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or double-handed grip, arms extended and spine straight (no rounded back).
instructions: Sit on the bench or seat facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent.
Select an appropriate weight, reach forward and grasp the handle with a neutral or double-handed grip, arms extended and spine straight (no rounded back).
Engage your core, maintain an upright torso (straight back), and initiate the movement by pulling your shoulder blades together (scapular retraction).
Drive your elbows straight back toward your torso while keeping them close to your sides until the handle touches your abdomen or lower chest.
Pause and squeeze your lats/upper back at the end range for 1–2 seconds while avoiding excessive torso lean or trunk rotation.
Slowly extend your arms and allow the cable to return to the start position under control, keeping a straight back throughout the eccentric phase.

exercises.description: A seated cable row performed with an upright, straight back to pull a handle toward the torso, emphasizing scapular retraction and elbow drive to work the mid- and lower-back muscles. Focus is on controlled pulling and resisting the cable on the return.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press

instructions: Set the pulley to the highest position and attach the pro-lat bar; select an appropriate weight.
Sit on the bench with knees secured under the pads and feet flat on the floor (seated position).
Reach up and grasp the bar with a wide overhand grip, hands slightly wider than shoulder-width.
Sit tall with a neutral spine and chest slightly lifted; lean back only a few degrees (small recline) — do not arch the lower back.
Initiate the movement by driving your elbows down and back, pulling the bar toward the upper chest/clavicle while leading with the elbows and squeezing the shoulder blades together.
Hold and squeeze the lats briefly at the bottom of the movement without leaning excessively backward.
Slowly and under control allow the bar to return to the start position until the arms are nearly fully extended and the shoulder blades re-expand.

exercises.name: Shoulder Grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grasp it with an overhand (palms facing away) grip approximately shoulder-width apart.
instructions: Stand under a pull-up bar and reach up to grasp it with an overhand (palms facing away) grip approximately shoulder-width apart.
Step or jump up so your feet are off the floor and your body is hanging straight down (dead hang) with arms fully extended; you may cross your ankles or bend the knees for comfort.
Engage your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to remove slack before initiating the pull (scapular pull).
Exhale and pull your elbows down and back, driving your chest toward the bar while keeping the torso as vertical as possible; lead with the elbows rather than the hands.
Continue pulling until your chin or upper chest reaches or clears the bar, squeezing the lats and upper back at the top for a brief pause.
Inhale and lower yourself slowly and under control back to a full dead hang with arms fully extended, maintaining tension in the lats and core.
Avoid kipping, excessive swinging, or shrugging the shoulders; keep movements controlled and use full range of motion for each rep.
If you cannot complete strict reps, use a band for assistance or perform negatives (slow controlled descent) to build strength.
exercises.description: A shoulder-width overhand pull-up that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi with upper-back and arm assistance. Begin from a dead hang and pull the torso upward until the chest approaches the bar, then lower under control.

exercises.name: Chin-Up
exercises.notes: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
instructions: Grasp a pull-up bar with a supinated (underhand) grip, hands about shoulder-width apart; hang from the bar with arms fully extended and feet off the ground (dead hang).
Brace your core and retract your shoulder blades slightly to stabilize the torso before initiating the pull.
Drive your elbows down and back by contracting your biceps and lats, pulling your chest toward the bar.
Continue pulling until your chin clears the bar or the bar touches your upper chest, keeping the torso relatively upright and avoiding excessive swinging.
Pause briefly at the top and squeeze the working muscles (biceps and upper back).
Lower yourself slowly and with control back to the full hang, extending the arms fully while maintaining shoulder blade stability.
Breathe out during the ascent and inhale during the controlled descent.
If you cannot complete a full chin-up, use a band-assisted variation, a box/step for negative-only reps, or an assisted chin-up machine to reduce load while maintaining the same body position.
exercises.description: A vertical pulling exercise performed from a hanging position with a supinated (underhand) grip that primarily loads the biceps while also working the lats and upper back. Pull your chin over the bar and lower under control to build upper-body pulling strength.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

Lie supine on the incline bench with your head supported, feet flat on the floor, and back slightly arched while maintaining scapular retraction.
Grip the barbell with a pronated grip slightly wider than shoulder-width; wrists stacked over elbows.
Unrack the bar by straightening the arms and moving the bar over your upper chest/shoulder line, then stabilize before starting the descent.
Inhale and slowly lower the bar in a controlled path to the upper chest/clavicular area, keeping elbows at roughly a 30–60° angle from the torso (avoid flaring to 90°).
Lightly touch or stop just above the chest, pause briefly without bouncing, then exhale and press the bar upward and slightly back to the start position, fully extending the elbows.
Keep core braced and feet planted throughout to maintain stability and prevent excessive lower back extension.
Carefully re-rack the bar under control or have a spotter assist when finished.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Upright Row
exercises.notes: Start standing upright (not kneeling or prone) with feet hip-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; hold a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated (palms facing body/slightly toward you) grip, arms extended at your sides.
instructions: Start standing upright (not kneeling or prone) with feet hip-width apart and a slight bend in the knees; hold a dumbbell in each hand with a pronated (palms facing body/slightly toward you) grip, arms extended at your sides.
Brace your core, pull your shoulder blades slightly down and back, and keep your chest up and torso vertical throughout the movement.
Initiate the lift by driving your elbows upward and outward, keeping the dumbbells close to the body so they travel in a straight line near the front of your thighs to chest level.

Continue pulling until your elbows reach about shoulder height or slightly above, with elbows higher than your wrists; avoid lifting the dumbbells higher than shoulder level to reduce shoulder impingement risk.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining tension in the delts and traps, then slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position under control.
Keep the wrists neutral (avoid excessive bending), avoid excessive torso swing or leaning back, and do not use momentum—focus on lifting with the shoulders and upper back.
Choose a weight that allows you to perform the set with good form for the desired reps; if you feel excessive shrugging or shoulder pain, reduce weight or limit range of motion.
exercises.description: A standing dumbbell movement where you pull weights up along the front of your body, driving the elbows upward to shoulder level to emphasize the delts and upper traps. Performed with a close grip and elbows leading, it also engages the biceps and forearms for stability.

tips: Focus on explosive concentric movements.

exercises.name: Cable seated row
exercises.notes: Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
instructions: Sit on the bench facing the cable machine with feet braced on the footplate and knees slightly bent (seated, upright position).
Grab the handle (V-bar or close grip) with a neutral or overhand grip and extend your arms so the weight stack is lifted and shoulders are protracted; maintain a neutral spine and chest slightly up.
Initiate the movement by retracting your shoulder blades, then drive your elbows straight back keeping them close to your body while pulling the handle toward your lower sternum/upper abdomen.
Keep the torso stable—avoid excessive leaning or using momentum; maintain a slight forward trunk angle only if required to reach the handle, then return to a tall, braced posture as you pull.

Squeeze the shoulder blades and lats at the end range for a brief 1–2 second pause, then slowly extend the arms forward under control to the start position while allowing the scapulae to move forward.
Breathe out while pulling and inhale while returning to the start; choose a weight that allows controlled form and full elbow extension between reps.
exercises.description: Seated cable row is a horizontal pulling exercise performed sitting with feet braced that primarily targets the lats while working the middle/upper back and arm pulling muscles. It emphasizes scapular retraction and elbow drive to pull the handle to the torso.

exercises.name: Barbell Bent Over Wide Grip Row
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor; keep your spine neutral and head in line with the spine.
instructions: Stand with feet about hip-width apart and place a loaded barbell on the floor in front of you.
Hinge at the hips with a slight bend in the knees until your torso is roughly parallel to the floor; keep your spine neutral and head in line with the spine.
Grip the bar with a wide overhand grip (wider than shoulder-width) and let your arms hang fully extended.
Brace your core, inhale, then pull the bar toward your lower chest/upper abdomen by driving your elbows out and back, squeezing your shoulder blades together at the top.
Pause briefly at the top with the shoulders retracted, then exhale and lower the bar in a controlled manner until arms are extended.
Repeat for the desired repetitions while avoiding torso rotation, excessive momentum, or spinal rounding; keep knees slightly bent and maintain the hip hinge throughout.
exercises.description: A bent-over barbell row performed with a wide overhand grip that emphasizes the upper back by retracting the shoulder blades and pulling the bar toward the lower chest/upper abdomen. The torso stays hinged forward while the hips and spine stabilize the movement.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.
