exercises.name: Dumbbell Standing Alternate Vertical Front Raises
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet shoulder‑width apart and a slight bend in the knees, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip).
instructions: Stand upright with feet shoulder‑width apart and a slight bend in the knees, holding a dumbbell in each hand at your sides (neutral grip).
Keep your core braced (abs engaged) and shoulders down and back to avoid upper back rounding or low‑back arching.
Begin the movement by slightly bending the elbow of the working arm and lifting the dumbbell straight up in front of you in a vertical plane (thumb leading or neutral), keeping the wrist neutral.
Raise the weight in a controlled motion until the arm reaches shoulder level or just above (eye/ear height), avoiding momentum or body swing.
Pause briefly at the top, then slowly lower the dumbbell back to the starting position under control.

Alternate arms, performing the same controlled lift on the opposite side while the other arm returns to the start; maintain an even tempo and steady breathing (exhale on the lift, inhale on the lower).
Do not use excessive weight that causes torso movement; if you arch your back or swing, reduce the load to preserve proper delt isolation.
exercises.description: A standing, single‑arm dumbbell front raise performed alternately to isolate the anterior deltoid while the opposite side rests. The exercise uses a controlled vertical lifting path to raise the weight in front of the body and slowly lower it back down.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Alternate Front Raise
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the edge of a flat bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and a neutral spine.
instructions: Sit upright on the edge of a flat bench with feet flat on the floor about hip-width apart and a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at your sides with a firm grip, arms fully extended and shoulders relaxed; palms can face your thighs (neutral) or rotate slightly so the thumb points up.
Brace your core and keep your chest up; avoid leaning back or using the legs to help lift.
Exhale and raise one dumbbell straight in front of you with a slight bend in the elbow, lifting to about shoulder height (arm roughly parallel to the floor).
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a tall posture and keeping the opposite arm stationary at your side.
Inhale and slowly lower the weight back to the starting position with control.
Repeat the movement with the other arm, alternating sides in a steady, controlled rhythm; avoid swinging the torso or using momentum.
Keep repetitions controlled (2–3 seconds up, 2–3 seconds down) and stop the lift before your wrist or shoulder becomes painful; breathe out on the lift and in on the return.
exercises.description: Seated alternate front raises involve lifting one dumbbell at a time from the thigh to about shoulder height while seated, primarily working the anterior deltoid. Maintain an upright posture and controlled tempo to isolate the front delts and minimize momentum.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Seated Close Grip Press
exercises.notes: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
instructions: Sit upright on a bench with back support, feet flat on the floor and core braced.
Pick up two dumbbells and hold them close together at chest/shoulder height with a neutral or slightly pronated grip so the heads are nearly touching and elbows point down and slightly forward.
Keep your elbows tucked close to your sides (not flaring out) and maintain an upright torso throughout the movement.
Exhale and press the dumbbells straight upward until your arms are almost fully extended, stopping short of locking the elbows.
Inhale and slowly lower the dumbbells back to the starting position at shoulder height under control.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining tight core and avoiding excessive arching of the lower back.
Common cues: keep dumbbell heads close together, drive through the triceps rather than pushing with the chest, and avoid flaring the elbows outward.
exercises.description: Seated dumbbell close-grip press is a pressing movement performed sitting with the dumbbells held close together at shoulder level; it emphasizes the triceps while also engaging the front delts and inner chest. Press the weights upward in a controlled motion, keeping the dumbbells close to each other and elbows tucked.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Keep rest periods consistent for metabolic stress.

exercises.name: Cable Seated Wide grip Row
exercises.notes: Sit upright on the bench/seat with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the wide rowing bar with a wide, overhand grip.
instructions: Sit upright on the bench/seat with feet braced against the footplate and knees slightly bent; grasp the wide rowing bar with a wide, overhand grip.
Start with arms extended, torso upright and chest up, maintaining a neutral spine and slight forward lean from the hips.
Initiate the movement by pulling the shoulder blades back and down (scapular retraction), then drive the elbows out and back to bring the bar toward the upper abdomen/lower chest.
Squeeze the upper back and hold the contracted position for 1 second, keeping the neck neutral and avoiding excessive torso lean or momentum.
Slowly return the bar to the start by extending the arms under control while maintaining an engaged core and neutral spine; inhale on the return, exhale during the pull.
Repeat for desired reps, keeping movements smooth and avoiding jerking or using the lower back to complete the pull.
exercises.description: Seated cable wide-grip rows train the upper back by pulling a wide handle toward the torso, emphasizing the mid-back and rear shoulder muscles while minimizing lower-back involvement. Use controlled scapular retraction and elbow drive to maximize upper-back engagement.

exercises.name: Assisted Parallel Close Grip Pull-up
exercises.notes: Set the assistance weight on the machine to provide the desired amount of help (more weight = easier).
instructions: Set the assistance weight on the machine to provide the desired amount of help (more weight = easier).

Kneel on the machine’s knee pad so your feet are off the ground and your hips are neutral; grasp the parallel (neutral) handles with a close grip (hands roughly shoulder-width or slightly narrower), palms facing each other.
Start with your arms fully extended, chest lifted, shoulders pulled slightly down and back (scapular depression/retraction) and core braced.
Exhale and pull by driving your elbows down toward your ribs, squeezing your lats and upper back, until your chin clears the handles or your chest approaches the bar.
Pause briefly at the top while maintaining a tight shoulder position (avoid shrugging), then inhale and slowly lower yourself under control back to full arm extension.
Maintain a steady tempo, avoid swinging or kipping, and adjust assistance as you get stronger to progress the exercise.
exercises.description: A machine-assisted neutral (parallel) close-grip pull-up that emphasizes the latissimus dorsi while reducing load with a counterweight pad to make the vertical pulling motion easier. The close, neutral grip increases biceps and forearm involvement and promotes a more upright torso.

exercises.name: Cable Standing Fly
exercises.notes: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
instructions: Set the cable pulleys to a high or slightly above-shoulder position and attach single-handle grips.
Stand centered between the towers in a staggered (split) stance or feet shoulder-width apart with a slight forward lean from the hips so you have stable balance; brace your core.
Grasp a handle in each hand with a neutral or palms-down grip and take one step forward until you feel light tension in the cables; keep a small bend in the elbows throughout.
Start with your arms out to the sides roughly at shoulder height (or slightly higher for a high-cable fly) and chest lifted; retract your shoulder blades slightly to protect the joints.
Exhale and bring the handles together in a controlled arc in front of your chest at midline, leading with the elbows and squeezing the pectorals at the finish.
Pause briefly with hands meeting or slightly overlapping, then inhale and slowly resist the cables as you return your arms to the start position using the same arcing path.
Keep the movement smooth and avoid locking the elbows, excessive torso rotation, or shrugging the shoulders; maintain core tension for stability.
exercises.description: Standing cable fly performed with the pulleys set high to mid-high engages the chest by bringing the arms together in a wide arc; the shoulders and core stabilize the movement. It emphasizes the pectoral fibers with controlled concentric adduction and slow eccentric return.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Maintain a steady rhythm throughout each set.

exercises.name: Lever One Arm Lateral High Row
exercises.notes: Adjust the machine seat and chest/back pad so the handle is reachable with your arm extended overhead while seated; select an appropriate weight.
instructions: Adjust the machine seat and chest/back pad so the handle is reachable with your arm extended overhead while seated; select an appropriate weight.
Sit upright with your chest braced against the pad, feet flat on the floor, core engaged and torso stable to prevent twisting.
Reach up and grasp the single high handle with one hand (neutral or pronated grip) while keeping the shoulder down and away from the ear.
Initiate the movement by driving the elbow down and back in a lateral arc, leading with the elbow so the hand moves toward the side of your ribs and the shoulder blade retracts and rotates downward.
Squeeze the upper back (scapulae) at the end range for 1–2 seconds while keeping the torso still and avoiding excessive shoulder elevation or rotation.
Slowly return the handle to the start position with control, fully extending the arm while maintaining tension in the working muscles.
Perform the desired reps on one side, then switch arms and repeat. Maintain steady breathing (exhale while pulling, inhale while returning).
exercises.description: A single-arm high pulley/lever row performed from a seated position that pulls the handle laterally and upward to emphasize the upper back and rear shoulder musculature. It focuses on scapular retraction and elbow-led pulling to target the upper back and lats while the arm and grip work the biceps and forearms.

exercises.name: Cable Low Fly
exercises.notes: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-hand handles.
instructions: Set both cable pulleys to the lowest position and attach single-hand handles.
Stand centered between the towers in an upright position with feet shoulder-width apart or one foot slightly staggered for stability.
Grasp the handles with a neutral grip so the cables run low behind your legs; step forward slightly to create tension in the cables and keep a tall chest.
Keep a slight bend in the elbows throughout the exercise (soft elbows) and a small forward lean from the hips; do not round the shoulders.
Exhale and pull both handles upward and inward in a controlled wide arc until your hands meet or nearly meet at chest level, squeezing the pectorals at the end of the motion.
Hold the peak contraction briefly, keeping the shoulders down and scapulae stable (use serratus anterior to stabilize the shoulder blades).
Inhale and slowly reverse the movement, allowing the cables to return your arms to the start position with control — do not let the weights slam.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions, maintaining posture, a consistent elbow angle, and steady tempo throughout.
exercises.description: Standing cable low fly performed with the pulleys set low; you pull each handle upward and inward in a wide arc to bring the hands together in front of the chest, emphasizing horizontal adduction to work the pectorals. Maintain a slight elbow bend and controlled tempo throughout the movement.

exercises.name: Cable Close Grip Front Lat Pulldown
exercises.notes: Attach a close-grip/V-bar or place hands close together on a narrow bar; grasp the handle with a firm, neutral/pronated grip (hands close together).
instructions: Sit on the lat pulldown bench and secure your thighs under the pads so your torso cannot rise during the movement (seated position).
Attach a close-grip/V-bar or place hands close together on a narrow bar; grasp the handle with a firm, neutral/pronated grip (hands close together).
Start with arms extended overhead, chest up and a slight lean backward from the hips (about 10–20°) to put tension on the lats (slight recline).
Initiate the pull by driving your elbows down and back, leading with the elbows rather than the hands, and pull the bar to the upper chest/upper sternum.
At the bottom of the movement, squeeze your lats and upper back for a brief 1–2 second pause while keeping the torso stable and not using momentum.
Slowly extend your arms back to the start in a controlled manner until your lats are under tension again (full but not locked extension).
Repeat for the desired number of reps; keep core engaged and avoid excessive swinging or pulling with the lower back.
exercises.description: Seated cable pulldown using a close grip to emphasize the latissimus dorsi while minimizing shoulder abduction; a narrow grip increases biceps and upper-back involvement. Performed seated with thigh pads to stabilize the body while pulling the handle to the upper chest.

tips: Control the eccentric (lowering) phase for 2-3 seconds.
Focus on the mind-muscle connection.
Every working set should reach true muscular failure.

Keep the chest up and shoulder blades stable; exhale and press the dumbbells straight up until the arms are nearly extended but not locked.

exercises.description: Seated dumbbell shoulder press with a parallel (palms-facing) grip; presses dumbbells from shoulder height overhead to work the delts while the triceps and upper back stabilize and assist.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Scott Press
exercises.notes: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and a neutral spine.
instructions: Stand tall with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart and a neutral spine.
Hold a dumbbell in each hand at chest level with the handles vertical, palms facing each other and elbows pointed forward (in front of the torso).
Brace your core and keep the ribcage down to avoid excessive lower-back arching.
Press both dumbbells upward in a controlled line until the arms are fully extended overhead, avoiding flaring the elbows too far out.
At the top, slightly protract the shoulder blades (push them forward) to engage the serratus anterior, then pause briefly.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back to the start position with elbows returning to the forward chest position.
Repeat for the prescribed reps, maintaining steady tempo and full control; stop or reduce load if you compensate by arching the lower back or shrugging the shoulders.

Press the dumbbells overhead and lower under control to work the delts and triceps with additional grip demand on the forearms.

exercises.name: Dumbbell One Arm Upright Row
exercises.notes: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in one hand with a neutral grip (thumb facing forward) and the other hand resting on your hip or at your side.
instructions: Stand upright with feet hip-width apart, knees soft and core braced; hold a dumbbell in one hand with a neutral grip (thumb facing forward) and the other hand resting on your hip or at your side.
Start with the dumbbell hanging at arm's length in front of the thigh, wrist neutral and shoulder relaxed.
Exhale and pull the dumbbell straight up by leading with your elbow, keeping the elbow higher than the wrist and close to the body; aim to raise the elbow to about shoulder height.
Keep the torso vertical — avoid leaning back or using momentum from the hips; do not excessively shrug the shoulder up toward the ear.
Pause briefly at the top while squeezing the delts/traps, then inhale and slowly lower the dumbbell back to the start under control.
Perform the desired number of repetitions on one side, then switch arms; maintain consistent tempo and joint positions throughout each rep.
Safety cues: stop the upward motion if you feel shoulder pain, do not raise the elbow above shoulder height to reduce impingement risk, and keep the wrist straight to avoid excessive bending.
exercises.description: Standing one-arm upright row: lift a single dumbbell by driving the elbow upward and out to emphasize the lateral delts and upper traps while keeping the torso upright. Perform controlled raises to about shoulder height and lower under control.

exercises.name: Dumbbell Bent Over Face Pull
exercises.notes: Hinge at the hips until your torso is bent forward (roughly 30–60° from horizontal), keep a slight bend in the knees and maintain a neutral spine and braced core (avoid rounding the lower back).
instructions: Stand with feet about hip- to shoulder-width apart holding a dumbbell in each hand with a neutral grip (palms facing each other).
Hinge at the hips until your torso is bent forward (roughly 30–60° from horizontal), keep a slight bend in the knees and maintain a neutral spine and braced core (avoid rounding the lower back).
Let the arms hang straight down from the shoulders so the dumbbells are below chest level and relax the shoulder blades.
Initiate the movement by pulling the elbows up and out (away from the body) so the upper arms move roughly parallel to the floor; lead with the elbows rather than the hands.

As the elbows reach about shoulder height, externally rotate the shoulders so the dumbbells move toward the sides of your face (palms may end up facing you or slightly upward), and squeeze the rear delts and scapular muscles together at the top.
Pause briefly at the top for 1–2 seconds, keeping the torso still and chest facing the floor.
Slowly reverse the movement under control, allowing the arms to extend back to the starting hanging position while keeping the torso braced.
Breathe out during the pull and inhale as you lower; choose a weight that allows strict form and avoid using momentum or excessive torso movement.
exercises.description: A bent-over face pull performed with dumbbells that targets the rear delts by pulling the weights toward the face while hinging at the hips to emphasize posterior shoulder and upper-back activation. It combines a high-row with external rotation to hit the posterior shoulder and scapular muscles.
